title: GMN and IMO test AI fuel tools in Mexico – Digital Ship
summary: This IMO-backed trial in Mexico uses AI to optimize vessel fuel consumption, but real-world impact remains unproven. The system monitors fuel use and offers operational advice, potentially reducing consumption, but could also be more "greenwashing". We await transparent data on tonnes of CO2 reduced and verifiable fuel savings before endorsing any AI-driven claims of decarbonization.
content:
A vessel trial in Mexico is putting AI-driven decision tools into daily ship operations. The project links digitalisation with fuel efficiency and emissions control for ship managers. This is a shift towards data-led performance at sea.

Shipowners are exploring ways to improve fuel efficiency while meeting emissions targets, and a new pilot in Mexico is testing how artificial intelligence can support that task in real operations.

Launched at Pichilingue Port, the project introduces an onboard system designed to support decision-making through real-time data and performance analysis. It forms part of the Global Maritime Technology Cooperation Centres Network Phase II programme, funded by the European Union and implemented by the International Maritime Organization.

The system integrates real-time fuel consumption monitoring, performance analytics, and operational planning support. For ship managers, the focus is on improving day-to-day decisions that affect fuel use, maintenance, and overall vessel efficiency.

The pilot was presented during a national workshop organised by the Maritime Technology Cooperation Centre for Latin America under the theme “Advancing Maritime Decarbonisation in Mexico: Energy Efficiency Technology and Innovation for a Sustainable Transition”. The event brought together maritime professionals to explore practical approaches aligned with the 2023 IMO greenhouse gas strategy.

The GMN Phase II programme is designed to test technologies in operational settings, generating data that can guide wider adoption across fleets. Beyond this pilot, demonstrations include port efficiency, wind-assisted propulsion, and retrofit solutions.

Digital tools, beyond reporting, are increasingly part of how vessels are run, with direct implications for fuel cost, emissions performance, and asset management.
